name: Topic-scoped moderation config (/topicconfig) wiring
botUpdateServiceIntegrationNotes:
No new repository or Prisma model needed. `ChatSettingRepository` (packages/data/src/chat-setting-repository.ts) is a generic per-(tenantId, chatId) JSON key/value store — it already fits `TopicScopedConfig<T>` perfectly because that type is itself "one base config + an overrides map keyed by topicId", so the WHOLE per-chat structure (covering every topic in that chat) is stored as a single JSON blob under one key (`"topic_lock_config"`), exactly like the existing `schedule_rules` / other keys already stored this way elsewhere in bot-update.service.ts (e.g. lines ~11360-11425). No per-topicId key fan-out is needed and no migration is required.

Five insertion points, in order, all inside apps/bot/src/bot-update.service.ts (I did not edit this file myself, per instructions — see snippet):

1. Imports: add `isLockType, isTypeLockedInTopic, LOCKABLE_TYPES, parseTopicScopeCommand, resolveTopicConfig, type TopicScopedConfig` to the existing `@superbot/module-security` named-import block (~lines 390-515). These are already re-exported from `modules/security/src/index.ts` via `export * from "./topic-scoping.js"` and `export * from "./content-locks.js"` — no new package export needed.

2. Module-scope helpers (`TOPIC_LOCK_CONFIG_KEY`, `TopicLockConfig` type alias, `EMPTY_TOPIC_LOCK_CONFIG`, `isTopicLockConfig` guard): place right after the existing `isGamificationMissionKind` const, just before `@Injectable() export class BotUpdateService` (~line 778-780). Type aliases can't be class members, so these must be module scope, matching the existing pattern of `RPS_EMOJI` / `PLATFORM_BAN_USAGE` / `isGamificationMissionKind` declared in that same spot.

3. Handler registration: add one entry (`name: "topic-locks.command"`) to the `botHandlers()` array, immediately after the existing `"locks.command"` entry (~line 1188-1191), following the exact same `{ name, handle: ({context, update}) => this.handleXxx(context, update) }` shape used by every other command handler in that array (e.g. `"locks.command"` → `handleLockCommand`).

4. Two new private methods (`getTopicLockConfig`, `handleTopicScopeCommand`): place directly above the existing `private async handleContentLock(` method (~line 3422), i.e. right after `handleLockCommand` finishes (~line 3339). `handleTopicScopeCommand` mirrors `handleLockCommand`'s structure 1:1 (parse → early-return on null/not-ok → require chatId → gate mutating actions behind `ensureConfigPermission(context, update, "topic-locks.config")`, matching how `"locks.config"` gates `/lock` — same safety posture, no new bypass, `show` stays ungated exactly like the existing `list` action does for `/locks`). Persistence goes through `this.chatSettingRepository` (already constructor-injected at line ~899, defaulting to `InMemoryChatSettingRepository` — no DI wiring change needed).

5. **The exact content-lock evaluator hook** (the "evaluateLocks" ask): inside the EXISTING `handleContentLock` method (~lines 3440-3453), the block that fetches `this.contentLockRepository.getLocked(...)` and immediately calls `evaluateLocks(update.content, locked as LockType[])` is the one and only place per-message content gets checked against locks. Replace that block with the version in the snippet: it still checks the chat-wide list first (unchanged behavior/priority), and only if that yields no violation does it additionally resolve the topic-scoped config for `update.chat.topicId` and run `evaluateLocks` again against just the types `isTypeLockedInTopic` reports as locked for that topic (filtered over `LOCKABLE_TYPES`). This is strictly additive: it can only make MORE content get locked in a topic than before (topic overrides add restrictions), it never removes or weakens the existing chat-wide `/lock` enforcement, admin bypass (`isActiveChatMember` check just above, unchanged) still applies first, and the rest of the method (delete message, audit, `recordRiskSignal`, reply text) is untouched.

Safety notes: mutating `/topicconfig lock|unlock|reset` all require the same admin-only `ensureConfigPermission` gate the chat-wide `/lock` command already uses (new permission key `"topic-locks.config"`, no existing permission weakened); `/topicconfig show` is read-only and ungated, mirroring `/locks`' existing ungated `list`. `reset` only clears that one topic's override (never the shared base), so it cannot silently disable the chat-wide locks. No destructive Telegram action (ban/mute/kick) is introduced by this wiring — the only enforcement action is the pre-existing `deleteMessage` + audit path inside `handleContentLock`, unchanged in shape, just fed a possibly-larger locked-types list.

Optional follow-up (not done, out of scope per task): if there's a central `/help` command-list, it isn't touched here since it wasn't identified as part of this task and editing unrelated help text risked scope creep.
risks:
1. Concurrency: apps/bot/src/bot-update.service.ts, modules/security/src/index.ts and packages/data/prisma/schema.prisma are being edited live by another process in this same session (git status showed uncommitted changes to app.module.ts, guardian files, etc., and modules/security/src/index.ts changed mid-task). The snippet above targets specific existing anchor lines/methods (handleLockCommand end, handleContentLock's locked/evaluateLocks block, the botHandlers() "locks.command" entry) — re-verify those anchors still exist at those approximate locations before pasting, in case the concurrent process shifted them.

2. Pre-existing, unrelated tsc errors: running `node node_modules/typescript/bin/tsc -p apps/bot/tsconfig.json --noEmit` currently reports 7 errors in packages/data/src/admin-decision-repository.ts and known-admin-repository.ts ("Property 'adminDecision'/'knownAdmin' does not exist on PrismaClient") — this is a stale generated Prisma client vs. schema.prisma drift from other in-flight work, NOT caused by this change. I verified my new code in isolation (temporarily added as apps/bot/src/__topic_check__.ts, typechecked via the same tsc invocation, then removed) and it produced zero additional errors — the 7 errors above are the full and unchanged list with or without my code present.

3. `/topicconfig` only ever fires inside a topic (parseTopicScopeCommand itself returns a "no-topic" usage error for the general thread), so there is no accidental global-lock bypass path — a chat with no topics enabled simply can't use this command, which is correct/intended per the module's own doc comment.

4. Storage growth: because the whole per-chat `TopicScopedConfig` (all topics' overrides) lives under one `chatSettingRepository` key, a chat with an unusually large number of distinct topics each with an override will grow that single JSON blob rather than being spread across rows — acceptable for the expected topic counts (tens, not thousands) per existing chat-setting usage patterns elsewhere in the file, but worth knowing if a chat ever has extreme topic sprawl.
